does: This paper introduces the Interclass Confusion (IC) test, a black-box evaluation method for machine unlearning that adversarially manipulates training data to detect when unlearning procedures fail to adequately remove learned information. The authors first establish necessary criteria for unlearning evaluation methods and demonstrate that existing approaches fall short of these standards. They also propose two analytically motivated baseline unlearning algorithms, EU-k and CF-k, which outperform several popular inexact unlearning methods. The work empirically demonstrates how adversarial evaluation strategies can reveal insufficiencies in unlearning procedures and characterize various phenomena in the unlearning process.
connects: This work extends the growing body of research on machine unlearning, which emerged in response to privacy regulations and concerns about data deletion post-hoc from trained models. It builds on prior inexact unlearning methods that approximate the computationally intractable problem of exact unlearning, and responds to limitations in existing evaluation approaches for these algorithms. The paper connects to adversarial robustness literature by using adversarial data manipulation during training as an evaluation strategy. It also relates to broader work on robustness evaluation in machine learning, drawing on the principle that evaluation methods should be rigorous and resistant to gaming by unlearning algorithms.
enables: This work enables more rigorous assessment of unlearning algorithm effectiveness, providing researchers with a principled adversarial framework for detecting when unlearning procedures inadequately remove training data influence. The IC test and proposed baseline methods could inform the development of stronger unlearning algorithms by revealing failure modes. This evaluation framework opens avenues for studying how different threat models and data corruption scenarios affect unlearning, and could extend to other domains involving data deletion requirements, such as federated learning and continual learning systems. The work also suggests future research into adversarial evaluation paradigms for other privacy-sensitive ML procedures.
